data.title: Bally's: A Value Trap At $17, Years Away From Planned Expansions
data.description:
• Even at $17, Bally's (BALY) is a value trap, with 19 casinos in 10 states. For $6 a share more, you can buy Caesars (CZR) with 50 properties and more than 65 million rewards members.
  • Near $10b in new projects announced (Chicago, New York, and Las Vegas), and a net debt of $3.72b, Bally's already has GPLI REIT financing its $1b Chicago project. More debt is likely as we near these sizable project starts.
  • Investors may be enticed by the prospect of buying 19 casinos in 11 states generating $2.4b TTM in revenues at $17, but the prospects for $10b expansion in already crowded markets don't work now.
  • Existing properties can't generate significant FCF to reduce debt over a reasonable duration.

A bit of history for context is necessary in a Sell call now and a watchlist advisory by 2Q 2026.

The origin of Bally's as a public gaming company goes back into the 1970s, when the company was a manufacturer of slot machines and arcade games. After several challenges by gaming regulators, it received a casino gaming license in New Jersey for its 1980 Park Place casino, renovated from the base of the historic Dennis in Atlantic City. Park Place, built off a strategy of focusing on slots, budget players, and location feed from nearby Sands, made tons of money for the parent, Chicago's Bally Manufacturing Corp.

I joined the company as Senior VP of marketing operations in 1988, responding to an offer from its then President and CEO Richard Gillman, a former stockbroker involved in the original IPO. Gillman presided over a tight, profitable operation until his strategy failed as AC matured in the late 80s. Bally's hired me to oversee a tough transition to keep Bally's Grand (Golden Nugget) high-end play (bought from Wynn (WYNN) in 1987). At the same time, they wanted to add mass play using the Bally's formula. It was heavy stress on rewards for slot players, with little effort pursuing VIP.

The slot machine business nationally did well in that era of expanding legal gaming in ever more states and tribal outlets. But overall, among Bally's key strengths was its determination to keep debt workable in terms of cash flow. Analysts who liked Park Place stock were always concerned about debt.

Financing moves, like the acquisition in 1987 of Wynn's (WYNN) Golden Nugget, was easy now. Out of the blue came an arcade game first - a video game called Pac-Man and later Ms. Pac-Man. A variable from a Japanese game, Pac-Man exploded from day one in fall 1980, reaching $1B in sales. (Note: the manufacturing business and casinos were separately traded stocks.)

By the 1990s, Bally's Pac-Man arcade game alone reached $2.5b in revenue. It became a cultural phenomenon. By 2016, its accumulated revenue since introduction had hit $14b. This tidal wave of cash was the fuel that enabled it to grow. Ultimately a sluggish stock price in 1990 attracted trucking entrepreneur Arthur Goldberg, and finally it was bought by current Asian hedge fund operator Soo Kim's Standard General. To the industry, the Bally assets were deemed meh, per se, but Kim had a wider vision clearly sprung from the history of the company before him. It was the operating assumption that the Bally's brand had resonance with the gambling public. He paid Caesar's (CZR) $20 for the brand name. He has since applied the goal to bring Bally's up to tier-one casino chains by infusions of debt.

That view was not shared by industry leaders, and there was virtually no interest among top-tier casino operators in adding Bally's properties. That's how Soo Kim, with limited assets, scooped it up.

Above: Chicago project. Already crowded market in a difficult urban setting.

Enter Soo Kim: A hedgie from Asia's Standard General fund with under $671m in AUM, he began accumulating $20m of Bally stock in October 2020. What he saw in Bally's was the long tail of a company struggling out of a manufacturing sector to gain a footprint in casinos since 1980. And to an extent, post-Pac-Man, it went bumping along at the second-tier chain with a nice footprint in Vegas, with the rebranded Horseshoe it acquired from Caesars. By February, Soo Kim completed the transaction by paying $4.6b for total control. The company had a long legacy of a transactional mentality, which fit Soo Kim's goals.

From the get-go, the plan was to resurrect the existing casinos at bargain prices, resurrect its once more visible brands, and use debt to finance expansion. The bet is that investors in the sector see the value of 19 casinos, solid operations, cats and dogs included, as far more than $17 now. In January of this year, the stock reached a high of $19.70.

Knowing the company for more than 39 years, I don't see a rationale for its significantly gaining market share in existing markets. And its three mega projects planned are all located in high oversupply metro markets.

There are investors who believe BALY can hold its own and find real value at $17, but I don't at this time. The brick and mortar casino sector will be fine toward 3Q 2026 or earlier, but until then, there are too many headwinds to endure for a heavily indebted, tier-two casino group. Most of the properties are located in mature casino markets with short-term growth stymied by current macro headwinds.

Above: Past initial bullish outlook.

The perceived value at $17 is weak vs. a buy of Caesars at $23. BLY has 7m members vs. CZR's 65m, for example.

The presumed growth engine is in the very early stages of development. Bally's won the license for downtown Chicago, now financed by the Gaming & Leisure Properties REIT (GLPI) at $1.1b. At the earliest, I see accretive EBITDA not rolling into the parent until the early 20230s.

So what's driving investors now to either sit on their Bally's positions, open new ones because it appears cheap, or accumulate and price average? My view holds that it's a weak case for a hold now with so many opportunities for buy on the dip moves for companies like Caesars.

1. BALY's existing properties are entirely dependent on the recovery of the sector. At best it can inch up while others in the market increase shares.

2. I believe the financial picture is challenging. It can hold shares at a second-tier level, throw off minimal EBITDA, and slowly reduce debt. That's a long tail trek at the end of which can't promise much upside.

3. If any of the three big projects makes it to a debut by 2030, we could see a far more interesting upside. But that's five to seven years away. Until then, the $17 is kind of dead money vs. other real bargains in casino stocks.

4. If Bally's appears to have bet right on its long-term plans, it could begin to look interesting at some point. For example, the Chicago project becomes accretive to earnings by 2030. That's why it makes sense to watchlist the stock before that debut. Winning one of the three downstate New York licenses for the Bronx project enters a market that will be swelled up with a mega-resort built by another New York bid winner, $20b net worth hedgie Steve Cohen, as part of his NY Mets MLB holdings in Queens. Add to that an expanded Genting Queens racino (the third winner) and hotel long in place backed by deep-pocketed Genting of Asia, and finally New York's existing casinos: MGM Yonkers and Genting's Queens racino, and you would have a vastly underserved market for the Bally's Bronx project to compete with.

Bally's plan for a mixed-use casino and residential development on the site of the always troubled Las Vegas Tropicana confronts the same big question: Why? Does Vegas need more casino space? Savvy investor Tillman Fertitta had a grand plan for a Vegas project he has now abandoned. He keeps his 12.3% position in Wynn shares because of its wider scope, including Boston and, in development, the Al Marjan property on the Arab Gulf Coast.

Bear in mind when first announced, the entire industry pounced with bids for New York downstate, including Wynn, Las Vegas Sands (LVS), and MGM Resorts (MGM). After the initial enthusiastic rendering of fabulous mega proposals, one by one they withdrew, leaving the three final bidders with approval. It was, in truth, like getting a participation trophy in Little League for the three winners.

Summary: Our thesis is simple: $17 slipping down or inching up in the same price range at this time in the sector is dead money. It's at best a second-tier casino operator, short on funds - but still able to access partners like GLPI. A strong recovery of the sector in 2026 will be a plus with a small upside. My view now is that there are far superior assets in the sector available at bargain prices, some of them close to Bally's $17.

My call is to sell now before a negative quarter or two drives the shares below $12 if the recovery is stalled. Then watchlist the stock as the Chicago project nears completion to tease out what will be bullish estimates for its debut. Also bear in mind that under certain circumstances, a sell-down of the company's underperformers could be an option to raise cash. So it's a watchlist stock at that point.

For now, move the Bally's money to CZR for far better potential returns.

data.title: Richtech Robotics: Political Catalysts And A Lot Of Hope
data.description:
Richtech Robotics (NASDAQ:RR) made news with a big 18% jump on Dec. 3rd following news stories claiming that the Trump administration is going to put robotics firms in focus. Commerce Secretary Lutnick has been privately meeting with robotics companies, and the market believes that Richtech Robotics is going to be one of the winners in this policy shift.

The big news is that the administration is considering an executive order, much like what it did for the nuclear power industry earlier this year. That was a great catalyst for many names in the industry, especially small and mid-cap companies. We're seeing the same thing in RR now that robotics are in view.

The US Wants to Take On Robotics

The impetus for this political will in boosting robotics is in competition with the Chinese manufacturing sector. China has a significant number of robots compared to the US (some estimates say easily double, if not triple) and has factories that are so automated they keep the lights off, since the robots use infrared cameras ("IR") to "see" and humans watch from IR in a separate building when they need to check on the factory. They call these "dark factories." I recommend this quick Wall St. Journal video about them if you'd like a primer.

We already have some factories that operate close to human-less in the US. Take Tesla's (TSLA), Nevada facility, which operates 90% autonomously, but nothing at the same scale as some Chinese factories. This makes it difficult to compete in the sector, and is one of the reasons that manufacturing in the US has been declining for some time.

It's impossible for me to believe that we're serious about "bringing manufacturing back to the United States" until we fix these statistics. Our production hasn't meaningfully changed in twenty years, and our capex today is below that of the 90s.

We will see if this new executive order and potential legislation is meaningful to these statistics, but that will take time. The timelines shown above are immense, and span decades.

This isn't a problem that was created in a day, nor is it solvable in one.

It's unclear to me if we're capable of solving this in the long run. Even if this administration is successful in their efforts, it will take several successive administrations, all focused on the same goal, in order to actually achieve real change. Time will tell on that front.

What is clear is that solving the manufacturing stagnation in America cannot be done without robotics and automation. Advanced manufacturing like semiconductors requires care and precision, and assisting specialized humans requires a lot of grace, things that many bots of the past lack.

Enter Richtech Robotics

RR is one of the firms building this new generation of advanced robots, with its hands in both industrial and commercial ventures currently. Robots-as-a-service is projected to be a major industry, though it will likely have few winners. Investors see RR as one of those potential winners, because they are reaching into multiple industries with generalist robots.

While I am skeptical that much of the stock is built up on hype, the actual business seems very real, albeit small.

The flagship robot that has already been deployed is a drink mixer, more or less. ADAM is the "AI-powered bartender, barista, and boba maker that wows your customers and boosts your bottom line," according to RR's website. I am not all too impressed with these kinds of robots, mostly because I see these kinds of robots as showpieces and not real game-changers for businesses.

Maybe I'm wrong, but the TAM for people who want a robot bartender to serve them drinks (more than just at a one-time event where it's a novelty) is fairly small compared to industrial needs.

Here's where things get interesting for me, the industrial bots.

The company's newest humanoid robot is called Dex, and it is supposed to offer firms a whole different kind of automation experience. It was debuted in late October this year, and has not seen any real deployment as far as I can tell. It's likely too soon to have fulfilled orders, to be fair.

They boast that Dex "fits into the systems you already use." This is the major selling point for many manufacturers and industrial firms that have struggled to use legacy automation tools and robots, because they were typically too bulky and too hyper-specialized (or else they were not cost-effective) to plug straight into a business. Entire factories needed to be refitted and rebuilt, causing time delays and imposing costs that many firms simply refused to shoulder.

Humanoid robots like Dex promise to fix this dilemma, which is the largest selling point for them.

Here are Dex's specs. Note that it doesn't use IR like many legacy robots or those that work in dark factories, but instead a combination of sensors using "real" vision, i.e., RGB and depth cameras.

While Dex and ADAM are on much of the marketing on their website, RR builds many kinds of robots. The one I am most interested in is their other industrial robot, Titan, built to assist humans (or robots like Dex) by delivering things across factories with many moving parts. These are similar to the kind of robots Amazon (AMZN) uses in its warehouses, but Titan's ability to navigate complex and constantly changing paths with humans in them could be a game-changer for many businesses.

Here is RR's full lineup:

Current deployments are very small. There is a BMW (OTC:BMWKY) dealership in Plano, Texas that they have deployed Titan at. RR has a promotional video on the deployment. They call it a "case study" on their website, but it's 2 minutes long, so I refuse to repeat that in any seriousness.

Otherwise, it seems like most of the deployments so far have been to one-time corporate events. While good for exposure and investor interest, RR has been unable to materially affect its bottom line yet with robotics deployments.

That's expected, since the company is in its early stages, but it is definitely something to consider when investing now. We are not at the point yet where they can claim large-scale success in deployment.

A Classic "Innovation Co" By The Numbers

I have long had a wishy-washy relationship with "innovations cos," or firms that make (and even deliver!) new and high-tech products/services, but fail to do it profitably. This combination often leads to stock price charts that look like rollercoasters, because their valuation is largely built on hope for the future and a promise of future earnings, not current earnings growth.

Here's RR's price and EPS since it came to the market a few years ago; they are a perfect example of this market dynamic.

Revenue is not growing, and operational expenses are very high. Again, this is classic innovation co territory, and I don't expect them to become more profitable for some time. Economies of scale needs to take place, and more robot-as-a-service deployments allows for more depreciation, which would improve the bottom line.

Where is the money coming from to cover all of this? The answer is unfortunate for current shareholders, but usually worthwhile for most firms to engage in, especially when their stocks are as bid up and hyped as RR's has been: stock issuance.

As the company needs money, they have been issuing shares to cover those costs. This comes at the expense of current shareholders, which are diluted by the issuance of these new shares. Consider that since its Jan. 2024, RR has more than tripled its share count.

That being said, I believe that for innovation cos like RR, this is often better than taking on debt. This is something that I like about RR's balance sheet; they are very low on liabilities. Very little long-term debt, which was mostly spent on their new Nevada facility.

Their liabilities to assets ratio is very low, meaning that they may be able to sustain their firm for a long time. They have been working to reduce liabilities in general, down from $4M to less than half that today.

A solid cash runway is vital for the survival of these firms, which need to outlast their competitors and last long enough in the market so that the massive spending they are doing on R&D pay off. R&D takes time, and many speculative ventures like robotics firms can't last long enough for it to pay off.

In a company like this, we should expect to see that kind of R&D spend, but without profits to fund it, keeping tabs on its level is important.

Calculating the actual runway they have is difficult because it will cost Richtech very little to dilute shares and raise more cash at any time. This is a good problem to have, but note that it makes estimating how long their cash will last a game of cat and mouse.

From their last 10-Q, here is what we can expect:

Cash & Equivalents: $32.893M

Short-term Investments: $52.616M

That brings our core liqudiity to ~$85.5M.

Annualized operating expense total $14.9M, extrapolated from a 9-month trailing operating expesnes of $11.197M.

Based on these current numbers, Richtech looks to have a little over two years in OpEx covered by their cash, or five years if also using their short-term investments. Adding in growth-oriented capital expenditures, which total $4.86M, lives us with a four year runway.

Of course, this is likely to change over time, as firms at this stage often do not have steady expenses or revenue. However, the current figures paint a good picture. Not a lot of firms this size have four years of their core business expenses on hand, and fewer have that and less than their liabilities in assets.

Conclusion

It's always difficult to rate companies that operate as a loss because the real power behind the stock is currently narrative and technical, not fundamentally driven. There is a lot of promise in Richtech Robotics, both from the company itself and from the government, regarding potential interventions that would benefit the robotics market and thus the company.

However, almost all of that is currently hype and hopeful foresight. That can be very powerful, but also result in heavy losses. The stock's history should be very clear on that point. Investors also run the risk of further stock dilution, which could cause shares to be devalued, even if they are correct that RR becomes more profitable over time.

Buying RR today is a bet that it will become profitable in the future, because there are no earnings to speak of today. It's a bet that its products will be useful for re-industrialization efforts in the US. It's a speculative bet, and one that could go very poorly. RR has a long road ahead of it, but one that is worth watching.

For now, I am giving the firm a hold rating, but I am adding it to my watch list and will keep tabs on it. The stock is too bid-up currently, especially since the most recent jump was based mostly on rumors and the anticipation of future political intervention. Without any details on that intervention, the buying pressure is mostly hype.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Microsoft: Ignore The Anti-AI Chatter
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) was leading all hyperscalers in the first half of 2025, like I said it would in my initial coverage of the stock.

Unfortunately, it lost its lead in the second half of 2025 as Alphabet (GOOG) took over as the clear leader, outperforming the hyperscaler complex for the full year.

The thing is that, since we launched our portfolio in late September, The Pragmatic Optimist Portfolio, we have allocated the highest amount of capital to Microsoft, investing over 12% of our initial capital in the stock.

However, the stock has been under pressure since its Q1 earnings amid a shift in the “AI trade” narrative in November as well as growing competitive concerns over Google’s latest Gemini 3 model launch.

Not only that, two days ago, OpenAI (OPENAI) announced "code red" to improve the quality and reliability of ChatGPT, especially as Google's Gemini models have leveled the playing field. FYI: Microsoft is OpenAI’s largest external investor with a 27% stake in the company.

To make matters even more complicated, news emerged yesterday that Microsoft has lowered sales quotas for their AI products after several sales staff missed their goals in the fiscal year that ended in June, per The Information.

While Microsoft management issued a statement denying the accuracy of the report, it has undoubtedly ushered in uncertainty amid “AI bubble” fears. There is no doubt that OpenAI’s success is important for Microsoft’s business strategy. However, Microsoft has been diversifying by investing in rival AI labs like Anthropic (ANTHRO) and Mistral AI, while developing their own in-house AI models and custom silicon. Finally, there is no reason to become bearish on OpenAI just because they announced “code red.”

The truth is the short-term volatility in stock prices is just a function of the changing narrative. When it comes to the AI landscape, narratives can flip very fast. In this case, I continue to remain bullish on Microsoft as an existing investor over a 3-year investment horizon. Having said that, I may be trimming my position sizing in the stock at certain levels in order to better manage risk in my portfolio.

Microsoft’s Q1: What AI Capex Slowdown?

Out of all the hyperscalers, Microsoft had the most muted price action in its stock price after its Q1 FY26 earnings at the end of November, despite beating both its top and bottom lines.

During the quarter, Microsoft Cloud revenue surpassed $49B, growing 26% YoY, with their commercial RPO (Remaining Performance Obligations) up 50% YoY to $400B with an average duration of 2 years.

Out of this, Microsoft Azure and Cloud Services revenue grew 40% (100 basis points higher than the previous quarter). This is compared to a 34% YoY growth rate in GCP (Google Cloud Platform) and a 20% YoY growth rate in AWS (Amazon Web Services) (AMZN). However, investors were not particularly impressed, as management projected a slight slowdown in their Azure growth rates next quarter to 37% YoY.

Note, since the last time I wrote on Microsoft, we got the much-needed clarity into Microsoft and OpenAI’s new agreement that was released before the Q3 earnings, where Microsoft will now own 27% of the new OpenAI PBC (valued at $135B), with a $250B Azure commitment and commercial rights to OpenAI models extended through 2032.

What was also striking was that Microsoft management, who had earlier projected for their pace of capex growth to slow in FY26, did an about-turn, where they now expect their FY26 capex to grow at a faster rate than FY25. In Q1, Microsoft reported capex of $34.9B, with analysts projecting full-year capex to exceed $120-$125, especially after taking the $250B commitment from OpenAI into account. As I discussed in my previous post, Microsoft’s CFO Amy Hood has made it clear that she is not particularly focused on picking a date when revenue growth and capex will converge and instead is focused on building a backlog and delivering capacity to meet their clients’ workload requirements.

Breaking down OpenAI’s Code Red

With Microsoft projecting their capex at a faster rate in FY26 than the previous year, let us not forget that Microsoft remains OpenAI’s largest external investor with a 27% stake in the company. In simple terms, this means that OpenAI’s success is extremely important for Microsoft’s overall business strategy.

So, when OpenAI’s CEO Sam Altman announced “code red” two days ago, investor pessimism further deepened in Microsoft stock, which had already been under pressure in the month of November from a combination of a general worsening of sentiment in the AI trade along with Google releasing their new Gemini 3 models that received much praise from the market.

Now with OpenAI announcing “code red,” it is fairly obvious that the main reason is attributed to rising competition from Google’s Gemini, especially after notable industry leaders.

I also wanted to share a couple of charts from the Financial Times, which show that Gemini app users may be more engaged than those of other rival chatbots, while Gemini’s app seems to be catching up to ChatGPT’s.

Note that these charts are from before the Gemini 3 launch. Not to mention, Marc Benioff’s (CEO of Salesforce (CRM)) statement that he is switching from ChatGPT to Google’s Gemini 3 created quite a stir.

I want to make it clear. It’s not like I don’t believe that OpenAI can come out of this stronger. However, what makes matters slightly complicated is that OpenAI’s financial plans involve tremendous levels of spending on AI infrastructure of $1.4T in data center and infrastructure commitments over the next 8 years. This is obviously not the case with Google, which is sitting on $75B in cash, more than OpenAI has raised in its entire existence.

Analysts at HSBC project that the company will need to raise an additional $207B by 2030 to sustain operations. While OpenAI expects to grow their revenues from an estimated $20B in 2025 to over $100B in 2029, any hint of a potential failure to reach those targets will create massive risks of not finding enough funding, which could create ripple effects in the AI ecosystem.

Microsoft Management Denied Having Lowered AI Sales Quotas

The good news is that Microsoft has been diversifying away from its exclusive reliance on OpenAI by investing in rival AI labs like Anthropic and Mistral AI, as well as developing its own in-house AI models and doubling down on its custom silicon roadmap.

Like I said in my earlier post, when OpenAI went on its deal-making spree in September with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Nvidia (NVDA), Oracle (ORCL), CoreWeave (CRWV), and more, with the Financial Times tallying it up to $1T+ worth of deals, Microsoft was missing from the party. The way I saw it, it was a strategic move on Microsoft’s part to expand and diversify their customer base beyond just catering to OpenAI’s gargantuan computing needs.

However, yesterday, news emerged that Microsoft had reportedly lowered how much salespeople are expected to grow annual sales of Foundry and other AI products after several sales staff missed their goals in the fiscal year that ended in June, per The Information.

While the stock naturally declined on the news, Microsoft issued a statement where they denied the accuracy of the report soon afterwards, stating that the story “inaccurately combines the concepts of growth and sales quotas, which shows their lack of understanding of the way a sales organization works and is compensated.

Revisiting My Valuation

While OpenAI’s “code red” does bring a layer of uncertainty, despite Microsoft diversifying its investments across AI labs while doubling down on its custom silicon roadmap, forward growth estimates have been revised higher over the last month.

On the revenue front, Microsoft is expected to grow its FY26 revenues by 16.01% YoY, and growth rates are expected to remain more or less anchored at these levels over the next two years to generate a total revenue of $435B.

Meanwhile, when it comes to earnings per share, expectations have also been revised higher, with growth rate at par or slightly higher than revenue growth over the next three years, as can be seen below.

What I would also like to point out is that Microsoft’s free cash per share has started to inflect higher on a TTM basis despite growing capex, which is generally a leading indicator of forward stock price returns.

The stock is currently trading at 30x its forward earnings per share estimates. Note that this is the same as that of Google, which has seen its price-to-earnings multiples expand over the last few months. But what is important to note is that Microsoft’s earnings per share are expected to grow at a faster rate than that of Google’s.

Assuming Microsoft’s price-to-earnings multiples expand to 36, which is roughly 2x that of the average S&P 500, given the growth rate in its earnings per share over the next 3 years, it should translate to a price target of $635 per share, representing an upside of 33% from current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

Microsoft is the victim of a change in narrative in the AI landscape at the moment. While OpenAI’s success is important to Microsoft’s business strategy given its capex projections, I believe Microsoft is strategically diversifying across rival AI labs as well as doubling down on its own custom silicon roadmap to better cater to their enterprise customers’ AI workloads.

It is hard to predict how long the period of uncertainty will last. On a technical level, it is key for Microsoft to hold its 100 DMA, which will allow it to find support on a short-term basis.

Meanwhile, the underlying fundamentals remain intact.

As a result, I will reiterate my Buy rating on MSFT stock with an updated price target of $635 per share, representing an upside of 33% from current levels.

data.title: A Weaker Dollar As The Fed Moves Toward Neutral: 3 Calls For FX
data.description:
By Chris Turner, Global Head of Markets and Regional Head of Research for UK & CEE

ING's base call: Less restrictive Fed policy delivers softer dollar

Our baseline call sees the Fed cutting rates back to neutral somewhere near the 3.00/3.25% area. With the ECB not touching its policy rate during 2026, FX hedging costs for eurozone holders of US assets should fall further. Three-month hedging costs have already fallen to 1.85% per annum from 2.45% as recently as July and are expected to fall further. Increased hedging activity should weigh on the dollar as it did in April this year.

For reference, the recent ECB Financial Stability report highlighted that eurozone investors, as of the second quarter of 2025, held $3.8tr of US equities, $800bn of US sovereign debt and $1.5tr of other US debt securities. With debt investors typically dealing with lower prospective returns, it will largely be this community which we expect to raise dollar hedge ratios next year.

Our risky call: Risk of negative real rates hurting the dollar

If political pressure mounts on the Fed to cut rates, we will all definitely be talking more about real interest rates in the US turning negative again. Two-year real swap rates have dropped to just 0.75% now from a peak of 2.5% in 2023. Any suggestion that the Fed is taking rates to inappropriately low levels could see real US rates turn negative again and weigh on the dollar.

For reference, when the Fed attempted to reflate the economy after the pandemic, real rates were taken down to the -3% area, and the dollar was under broad-based pressure. Were US real rates to make it back to the -1% area in 2026, we could see the dollar some 5-7% lower from current levels.

Our bold call: QE would be a game-changer for the dollar

Were the nexus of the US Treasury and the Fed to somehow agree that yield curve control was a good idea, the resurgence of quantitative easing would weigh heavily on the dollar. Printing money to keep the long end of the Treasury market in check, while good for growth, would further damage the credibility of the dollar and lead to a much clearer return of the ‘risk on, dollar off’ trading environment seen in the decade immediately after the Global Financial Crisis.

In the absence of a crisis, we presume the ECB and the Bank of Japan would be operating with much more stable balance sheets than the Fed and that the euro and the yen would witness some meaningful outperformance against the dollar. Under this policy, the US administration could achieve two of its goals at the same time: stable funding levels for the US government and a weaker dollar.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: EIDO: Hasn't Been Flourishing, But There's Potential For A Change
data.description:
An Unremarkable Year So Far

The $333M-sized iShares MSCI Indonesia ETF (EIDO) is a passively managed ETF that tracks the MSCI Indonesia IMI 25/50 Index, which in turn represents 99% of Indonesia’s free-float market cap. It’s fair to say that EIDO, which has been around since 2010 and currently covers 84 Indonesian stocks in total (45% of which consists of giant-caps), hasn’t set the world alight in 2025.

To elaborate, this year it has barely moved and has only generated one-tenth of the returns that global equities have facilitated on average. Meanwhile, EIDO’s emerging market peers have surged ahead by almost 30% (on average) during the same period. Clearly, EIDO hasn’t been winning any popularity contests, but is there potential for this to change?

Well, let’s take a look at some of the key factors that could weigh on EIDO’s performance.

How Are Indonesian Banks Faring?

We’re flagging the conditions of Indonesian banks in particular because this sector alone accounts for over 40% of the portfolio (also, the top 3 holdings are all banks), and comfortably over 2x more than the next largest sector- materials. Clearly EIDO won’t be in any position to dazzle until conditions here improve.

It's fair to say that business for these banks isn’t necessarily in the most resilient state, with loan growth only hovering at around the 7% levels, which is a lot lower than the double-digit growth levels seen across much of last year. Note also that it is below the central bank’s FY forecasted range of 8-11%.

Subdued credit appetite means the volume of undisbursed loan facilities relative to the total approved credit ceiling is relatively high, accounting for almost 25%. Some of the tepid loan appetite is also self-inflicted as banks are becoming more cagey with the spike in different consumer loan NPLs (non-performing loans).

Diminished appetite for credit is quite concerning when you realize that the Indonesian central bank has already facilitated 150bps worth of cumulative rate cuts since September 2024, with the current rate of 4.75% at its lowest level in over 3 years! Prima facie, one would think that these banks could potentially get yet another helping hand as the central bank is likely to cut rates this month and again in Q1-26, with the benchmark rate poised to dip to 4-4.25%. The central bank feels emboldened to push on with rate cuts because inflation is well within its comfort range of 1.5-3.5% at 2.72%.

However, what’s interesting to note is that monetary policy transmission in Indonesia hasn’t been particularly effective so far, with these banks only passing on 15bps points of reduction (out of the total 150bps cut since September last year). This is largely a function of Indonesian banks trying to stay competitive with the rates they are offering on their cost of funding (savings bank deposits, for example), and thus their desire to maintain some stability on their net interest margins [NIM]. For instance, take a look at the NIM% of EIDO’s top holding, PT Bank Central Asia (OTCPK:PBCRF), which has a hefty weight of 18% in EIDO. The NIMs have barely moved from a year ago at around 5.76%. This is, of course, a silver lining for bank profitability, as, even though loan growth isn't kicking off, stable NIMs are still providing a useful foundation for better profitability.

What About The Indonesian Rupiah?

So clearly interest rate cuts at the central bank level haven’t been making any meaningful impact on the loan affordability front, but they have proven to be quite damaging from a currency perspective, with the IDR proving to be one of the worst-performing emerging Asian currencies in the current year.

A weakening IDR against the USD is not great news for holders of EIDO, as EIDO is denominated in USD, but its holdings are denominated in IDR. Thus, even if these holdings fare well in local currency terms, if the currency depreciates against the USD, you may not get a meaningful uplift. The Indonesian central bank’s ability to support the currency shouldn’t be taken for granted, as foreign exchange reserves have dropped to their lowest level in 14 months.

Interestingly enough, longer-term there appear to be some efforts to boost the credibility of the IDR by redenominating it, but the bill that is supposed to address this will only be finalized by 2027.

What About Indonesia’s GDP Backdrop?

Well, GDP growth too isn’t in the pink of health, with the Q3 rate coming in at a little over 5% and putting a lot of pressure on Q4 to help reach the government’s target of 5.2% for the full year (growth in Q1 was only 4.9%, whereas Q2 had improved to 5.1%).

Investors can afford to be a little optimistic about GDP growth prospects going forward because in September, the Indonesian government unveiled a stimulus package to the tune of $1B, which is likely to be extended to even 2026.

How Is EIDO Positioned Versus Other Emerging Markets?

Prima facie, if you’re someone who attaches credence to the theme of long-term mean reversion in the financial markets, you may be interested to note that EIDO’s current relative strength ratio versus other emerging markets is at record lows and trading around 40% lower than its long-term mean (thus potentially raising the prospect of some normalization going forward).

However, for this to be a truly compelling trade, one would also need the valuation and earnings growth differential between these pockets to be meaningful enough, and we don’t necessarily feel that is the case here.

Yes, EIDO is priced at less than 13x P/E, which works out to a 10% discount versus emerging markets, but one could argue that the cheaper multiple is also a corollary of EIDO’s holdings having a much lower long-term earnings growth profile. At only around 5%, EIDO’s long-term earnings growth profile is not even half as good as what emerging market stocks are poised to see (close to 11%).

What Do EIDO’s Technicals Suggest?

EIDO may not have facilitated a great YTD performance, but developments on EIDO’s short-term daily charts since September/October suggest that this ETF is likely stoking the interest of momentum investors at this juncture. Note that since bottoming out in September, it has been witnessing a pattern of higher highs [HHs] and higher lows [HLs], while since October, it has also been trading above its key daily moving averages of 50, 100, and 200.

Switching over to EIDO’s long-term weekly chart, we feel the risk-reward at this juncture looks roughly fair. If one connects the peaks and troughs of EIDO’s weekly movements over the past 5 years (the two black lines), it appears that the risk-reward now looks roughly fair.

Closing Thoughts

EIDO hasn't had the greatest year, and a long position in this ETF isn't our most compelling idea. However, given the stimulus and monetary policy support, a potentially better Q4 from a GDP perspective, cheap valuations, mean-reversion prospects within the EM universe, and ongoing momentum on the short-term charts, with fair risk-reward on the long-term charts, we think there's enough in the tank to support a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brace For A Slowdown: 3 Calls For Global Trade
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

ING's base call: Global trade to slow

Due to AI-related trade and all the frontloading we saw ahead of the introduction of US tariffs, global trade has held up relatively well this year despite the highest tariff rates since the 1930s. Even though a collapse of global trade was avoided, US tariffs are here to stay. If anything, there is the risk of an escalation, rather than a de-escalation, of ongoing trade tensions. Not only is the US administration using tariffs as an important tool for all kinds of policy goals, but the US government also needs the revenues to partly fund the budget.

Another reason for trade uncertainty is that the current trade truce between the US and China will expire next year, potentially bringing new turbulence in the second half of 2026. Also, there is a significant risk that US trading partners will not be able or willing to deliver on their investment commitments under the trade agreements, which in turn could trigger renewed tariff action by the US government. Even though the US administration recently dialled back on some food import tariffs, we expect global trade to slow down significantly, from around 2.5% in 2025 to some 0.5% in 2026.

Our risky call: Accidental tariff acceleration

Despite Trump's assertion that 'tariff is the most beautiful word in the English dictionary,' the effective tariff rate in the US had turned out to be much lower than everyone feared after the President's 'Liberation Day' announcements. In the end, they've been an important element of the administration’s very transactional policy agenda. This suggests that the US government has no interest in completely melting down global trade but rather in reshaping it. In this regard, the US Supreme Court’s ruling on some US tariffs could trigger an unintended acceleration of said tariffs.

If the court rules against the tariffs, the US government may revert to more sectoral tariffs. These could do more harm not just to exporters but also to US consumers, triggering a much more adverse impact on the global economy.

Our bold case: The world can’t live without the US

Attempts by US trading partners to build new trade alliances all fail. In Europe, almost-finalised trade agreements like Mercosur don’t receive approval from some member states. The few new trade agreements that Europe manages to close all fall short of offsetting losses in exports to the US, as no other trading partner has the same purchasing power as the Americans. To make matters worse, while doubts about the rule of law in the US prevented many European companies from relocating, these concerns fade away, and Europe sees massive corporate emigration.

Consequently, and feeling the pressure to deliver on his election promises to bring back manufacturing jobs to the US, President Trump actually increases tariffs for European exporters ahead of the US mid-term elections. On top of this, the first review of the USMCA deal next year could also bring renewed tensions with Canada and Mexico.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Phinia Is Starting To Look Interesting Again
data.description:
This has been an “interesting” year for auto suppliers, as companies and investors have had to navigate confusing and often conflicting market dynamics, including an over-aged fleet in Western countries and improving affordability, but also tariff issues, OEM model lineup changes, and weak consumer confidence. Still, most of the sector has managed a 10% to 20% return so far this year.

PHINIA’s (PHIN) performance has been mixed. While the stock has done okay year-to-date (about average), the shares have lagged the likes of Forvia (OTCPK:FAURY), Lear (LEA), and Magna (MGA) since my last update in August, when I turned more cautious due to valuation and end-market challenges.

PHINIA has modestly outperformed my expectations since that last update, and I continue to like how the company is positioned for at least the next few years – the company is well-leveraged to ongoing fuel efficiency needs, as well as the growth of alternate fuels, and I like management’s active efforts into expanding its addressable markets beyond auto customers. This is honestly a toss-up call right now – I like the fundamental drivers, but the valuation still isn’t such that it’s an inarguable bargain.

Ongoing Solid Performance In Q3

PHINIA is still relatively new as an independent company (it spun out from BorgWarner (BWA) in mid-2023), but management is building a reputation for under-promising and over-delivering despite some challenging and uncertain end-market conditions.

The third quarter saw revenue improve about 5% in organic terms, beating by 4%, with fuel systems revenue up almost 10% and aftermarket revenue down less than 1%. While management does not provide quarter-to-quarter geographical splits, looking at past disclosures leads me to estimate that Ful Systems revenue outperformed underlying vehicle production growth in Q3’25 by about 440bp.

Gross margin was a bit weaker (down 30bp to 22%) on mix and cost pressures, while EBITDA rose 10% (margin up 30bp to 14.6%) and adjusted operating income rose 16%, beating by about 12%, with margin up 70bp to 11.1%.

At the segment level, profits rose 14% (margin up 80bp to 14%), with Fuel Systems up 33% (margin up almost two points to 13.3%) and Aftermarket down 4% (margin down 80bp to 15%) on an adverse mix. Management called out lower R&D as a boost to Fuel Systems margins, but it’s worth noting that PHINIA continues to spend more than average on R&D (a positive in my view).

Guidance Is Okay, But Likely To Drive Some Caution From Investors

While management did raise guidance for the full year, it didn’t line up with the magnitude of the outperformance seen in Q3, so the company effectively lowered guidance (modestly) for the fourth quarter.

Lower is not better, but I don’t think the guide is all that bad on balance. It looks as though reported revenue will be down around 1%, or around 2% organically, assuming that the revenue run rate for SEM is similar in the fourth quarter.

A 2% revenue decline isn’t great news, but with the S&P still looking for a nearly 3% decline in global passenger vehicle production in Q4’25, that’s not bad, particularly as I would expect Fuel Systems to outperform relative to Aftermarket. Likewise, the company is still on target for modestly better margins and cash flow than management originally projected for 2025, so I can’t say that performance is really trailing off all that much.

Still, the Street is nervous. The impact of tariffs is still open for debate, with suppliers looking to pass on costs, but OEMs haven’t always gone along with that. Likewise, if other OEMs follow General Motors’ (GM) lead in directing suppliers to move their sourcing away from China, there could be some operational wobbles that impact OEM production. There are also still concerns about affordability and consumer sentiment in both the U.S. and EU, and it remains to be seen if the dust-up between the Dutch government and China over Nexperia leads to any production disruptions.

Ongoing Efforts To Drive Growth Above Underlying Production Are A Positive

BorgWarner spun off PHINIA in part to focus its future on vehicle electrification, but that doesn’t mean that PHINIA is doomed to obsolescence. Not only are there multiple drivers that can extend growth in the existing/legacy auto business, but management is also actively looking to reposition its core technology into other markets.

It’s no surprise that EV adoption outside of China has come up short of analyst and industry expectations circa 2022, and that extends the life cycle for PHINIA’s Fuel Systems portfolio. Battery EVs are still well below 20% of the fleet in the EU (though they’ve recently reached that level for new sales) and even lower in the U.S. and Japan, and many auto OEMs have been refocusing on hybrids and alternate fuel systems in recent years. With hybrids and alternate fuel vehicles requiring direct injection and other related systems (like canisters and monitoring systems), this creates a longer runway of opportunity for PHINIA’s core offerings.

What’s more, while the current U.S. administration has moved to unwind fuel efficiency requirements that were finalized in 2024 and meant to go into effect from 2027-2029, consumers and state-level regulations can still push OEMs to improve fuel efficiency, which, in turn, drives more demand for direct injection and other higher-margin offerings from PHINIA’s portfolio.

Management has also been actively looking to extend its technologies into non-auto markets. The company presented at the Paris Air Show for the first time this year and has announced the first shipments to an aerospace customer. The company has likewise been developing agriculture and off-highway markets, announcing a recent win for brushless alternators for an Asian mining truck manufacturer and attending the Agritechnica show in November.

Aerospace OEMs can make use of PHINIA’s direct injection technology in piston-engine aircraft as a way to improve efficiency, power output, and emissions, and there are likewise applications for alternate fuel systems and new opportunities like drone propulsion systems where PHINIA can offer compact integrated solutions that simplify the design/build process while also driving advantages like improved flight time.

I’m also interested in seeing what could be possible for the company in power generation. While turbocharging doesn’t require direct fuel injection, it does allow for higher compression rates and more precise fuel control. With companies like Accelleron (OTCPK:ACLLY) seeing strong demand for turbocharging for gensets used as backup power supplies for data centers, this could give PHINIA a modest amount of leverage in this fast-growing segment, not to mention growth in alt-fuel generators for backup/peaking utility use.

The Outlook

PHINIA’s results to date have led me to modestly tweak my model, but the underlying fundamentals haven’t changed that much. I’m still looking for long-term revenue growth of around 2.5%, though there could be upside here if traditional gasoline, hybrid, and alt-fuel vehicles can maintain more share relative to battery EVs over the next 5-10 years. Likewise, there is still upside potential from management’s efforts to expand the company’s addressable markets (like aerospace and power gen).

I haven’t made significant changes to my margin assumptions. I’m still looking for long-term free cash flow margins in the mid-single-digits, driving mid-single-digit FCF growth. What I said above about upside to revenue from new markets applies here as well – if revenue comes in higher than expected, PHINIA should benefit from improved scale and operating efficiencies.

Between discounted cash flow and margin-driven EV/revenue and EV/EBITDA, I believe PHINIA shares are undervalued below the high-$50’s to low-$60’s and with a high single-digit long-term total expected return today.

The Bottom Line

In my mind, the upside in PHINIA shares and the ongoing risks in the auto industry make this a borderline neutral/buy call. I like what management is doing, and I like the opportunities in front of them to expand their addressable markets, revenue, and profits. For now, I’m sitting with “Neutral,” though I continue to own the shares, and this is definitely a name to consider if there’s a meaningful pullback from here without corresponding deterioration in the business.

data.title: XRPI: Futures Drag, Centralized Token And No Compelling Bull Case
data.description:
XRPI ETF Overview

We review the structure of Volatility Shares’ XRPI ETF, explain why, if I were to buy XRP, I would not choose this ETF but would prefer other spot ETFs instead, and finally why I consider XRP a token I would not invest in.

Volatility Shares’ XRPI ETF (XRPI) indirectly provides exposure to the XRP cryptocurrency via regulated XRP futures contracts (x1 exposure) listed on CFTC-registered U.S. futures exchanges (such as CME). This means that it is not a spot ETF and therefore the fund does not buy or hold XRP, and the exposure is always indirect, implemented through financial derivatives.

In their prospectus, they describe that at least 80% of its net assets will be invested in what they call “XRP-Linked Instruments” – mainly XRP Futures Contracts + swaps and other derivatives referencing XRP spot, XRP ETPs, etc.

On the fund's website you can find the main details, among which we highlight the expense ratio assumed by the investor. Until 30 June 2026, the fund offers an exemption on the 1.15%, leaving the cost at 0.96%. The cost is directly discounted from the NAV of the fund, so as investors the effect is “suffered” in a lower return.

Operations and Contango Risks

Operationally, the fund regularly rolls its futures contracts as they approach maturity. If the price of the new contracts is higher than those they have in possession (contango) at the time of the rolling, the fund sells “low” and buys high , this generates a cost that erodes the performance over time. Conversely, if the futures curve is downward-sloping (backwardation), at each roll the fund sells high and buys low, generating positive roll yield.

In their prospectus, they emphasize that persistent contango situations will negatively affect the fund’s performance and have a significant impact on the NAV.

That said, the fund makes money through three main avenues:

By appreciating the price of the underlying: if XRP goes up, so does the futures price. Rolling: whenever the curve is rolled in backwardation, the fund generates an additional positive return that adds to overall performance. Interest on T-bills

Holdings

At the time of writing, the holdings clearly show the characteristics and operation of the fund described above. Currently, the entire position is concentrated in a CME contract with maturity (Dec-25). The rest of the portfolio is composed of collateral and cash that provide liquidity and carry.

Distributions

In the same way that Volatility Shares has with other ETFs, XRPI distributes dividends on a monthly basis. Since its launch in May 2025, the minimum distribution has been $0.0125 per share and the maximum $0.0335. This variability is not surprising since they have a variable distribution policy that depends mainly on price revaluation (realized mark to market gains) and less on the income generated with the interest of the collateral and the carry of the roll. Also, distribution is not guaranteed and could be null.

Currently, the dividend yield stands at around 2.2%. In both tables, we can see how distributions increased over the summer, while the month-over-month change in the distribution turned negative in October and November:

  • Jun - Jul: +12.8%

  • Jul - Ago: +132.6%

  • Ago - Sep: +2.1%

  • Sep - Oct: -6.0%

  • Oct - Nov: -23.8%

This pattern confirms that most of the cash paid out depends on realized mark-to-market gains, which naturally shrink when XRP underperforms or corrects.

Peers

Recently, new XRP ETFs have been launched. To see if XRPI would still be the best alternative, let’s compare liquidity and AUM, expense ratios and dividends.

  • Type: From an operational point of view, any of the ETFs are a better choice than XRPI since spot trading is less expensive and complex than using derivatives to replicate asset movement.
  • Expense Ratio: All ETFs, just launched in November, except XRPC offer temporary discounts on the expense ratio. If we ignore this part, the most attractive would be XRPZ and clearly XRPI loses to everyone.
  • AUM: It’s still early, but XRP (Bitwise) and XRPC stand out for their ability to attract assets compared to the other ETFs. If they followed the trend, they would have an advantage over the others from a cost standpoint.
  • Dividends: XRPI is the only one that pays dividends.

If you're looking for XRP exposure and a recurring revenue stream, XRPI is your ETF right now. However, the effect of the dividends is diluted by a higher expense ratio and on the other hand, using futures instead of spot typically increases the fund’s tracking error versus XRP spot and, more importantly, introduces structural roll costs that tend to make long-term returns lag a spot ETF.

Finally, the recent news of Vanguard's change in its policy regarding crypto assets clearly favors ETFs that have opened the door such as: XRPC, XRP, XRPZ, GXRP, among others.

XRP Thesis

From a mainly fundamental point of view, I do not find the XRP token attractive as an investment.

XRP use case: For starters, XRP’s goal has always been to cover the inefficiencies of the current SWIFT system – that is, to replace it. This implies that its main utility today is cross-border payments, however in the face of regulations, token volatility, etc. Ripple already has its own stablecoin, RLUSD, which leads me to think that XRP will remain merely as a governance token and to maintain the operation of the network.

The blockchain network is highly centralized in a few nodes, and so is its governance. This means that, the control of the “laws” that govern the network is under the domain of a few and therefore, the ability to modify any rule of the network is very simple to apply, for example to increase the total number of tokens in circulation, to change the current release protocol of the tokens: this has already been done and, without going into much detail, in 2017 they implemented a program (escrow) that scheduled the release of 1 billion XRP monthly and recently (2025) changed the way in which those unlocks were executed.

On the other hand, there are no incentives for validators, that is, there are no rewards except for participating in the decisions of the “laws” of the chain, that is, in governance. There is no incentive to set up a node and this gives me the feeling of seeing it more like a private circle.

So I wonder: what could be the driver for an XRP revaluation? If their only use is to serve the functioning of the chain and governance? If it is also intended to be a system for cross-border payments between banks, they will not want to use a volatile token but their own stablecoin, RLUSD. That said, these payments would still run through its network, requiring XRP to process the transactions. However, the supply–demand dynamics would likely be very stable and, since no one directly benefits from the XRP used for transactions because the tokens are burned, I do not see in this relationship any strong driver of price appreciation. Instead, I have the feeling that XRP will end up being a very stable token, potentially trading permanently below one dollar.

On the other hand, the distribution of tokens is still very concentrated in a single “player”: about 42% is held by Ripple Labs, giving them significant power to move the price in their interests.

Of course, to speculate is entirely valid and there can always be volatility but personally I don't see any initiative and it's not a token that I would invest in. But if I did, I would choose one of the spot ETFs: XRPZ or XRP (Bitwise).

data.title: Why Alibaba May Be Winning The Global Model Race
data.description:
Introduction

Alibaba (BABA) has transformed into an AI-first organization. Today, Alibaba's Qwen is deployed on-site in multiple data-center facilities and on a wide variety of edge platforms, turning Qwen into the foundation for Alibaba's rapidly expanding AI footprint. Alibaba now offers some of the most powerful and forward-looking cutting-edge systems, and runs the leading AI cloud platform in China. Alibaba is developing the rail infrastructure for China's AI economy, and the rest of the company is being rebuilt to run on top of that network.

From E-Commerce Giant to AI Infrastructure Superpower

The clearest sign of Alibaba’s AI success is how aggressively it has reorganized the company around AI. The company views developing AI infrastructure as important to Alibaba's e-commerce business as it is for Alibaba itself. In addition, Alibaba’s revenue growth rates have been re-accelerating at their most rapid pace since last year.

Looking specifically at its cloud offerings, Alibaba is currently the top cloud platform in mainland China, with the largest overall share of China’s broader cloud market. According to analyst reports, Alibaba commands nearly 1/3 of all the cloud market in China, and approximately 35-36% of all the AI Cloud Market in China. As generative models mature, allowing the exposure of APIs to be run on purpose-built hardware, Alibaba is likely to be able to quickly establish itself as an AI leader.

Qwen: A Model Lineup That Closed the Gap

Alibaba has clearly signaled through Qwen that its days as a second-tier AI player are over. Relatively few organizations have moved to iterate at such a rapid pace while increasing how much they can actually deliver improvements in real-world performance. As early as the middle of 2024, the Qwen2 72B Instruct model was ranked within the top-10 best performing systems in China on the SuperCLUE benchmark for both text comprehension and software development. Alibaba can fairly claim that it created the most powerful open-weight model available in China.

Multimodal Depth

The most prominent and performant model in this fleet is Alibaba's trillion-parameter model, Qwen3 Max. In fact, benchmarks show it to be incredibly competitive with frontier US labs on most of the important metrics. Alibaba is focusing on developing the architecture needed for planning and multi-step decision-making so that the model can become even more performant. That shift is important for building models that can perform meaningful work.

The Qwen family has become a true multimodal family through Alibaba's efforts as well. The innovative "Thinker-Talker" design closely coordinates the audio and video streams for synchronization. The Qwen3 Omni extends these capabilities to virtual and augmented reality applications by integrating Alibaba's models into the next generation of interfaces. In addition, Alibaba is rapidly advancing toward the development of generative video technology via the Wan 2.1 model. This model is currently ranked atop one video leaderboard and will be available to developers on an open-source basis.

Open Weights, Developer Gravity, and Enterprise Adoption

Alibaba's true differentiator is in combining its closed models with an open-weight approach to its model ecosystem. Alibaba's Qwen has become one of the most active LLM ecosystems in the world by continually releasing new base and chat-tuned variants. As a result of this openness, there have been thousands of community fine-tunings and downstream applications developed. In fact, Hugging Face's Leaderboard currently lists several Qwen-based models at or very near the top. Hugging Face's CEO has publicly referred to Alibaba's 72B version of Qwen as the "King" among open models. This reflects how quickly Alibaba was able to close the gap between its models and those being used by Western leaders.

Qwen is also no simple research tool, as over 90,000 companies have already used Qwen-based solutions. The high volume of enterprise usage and the resulting embedding into business processes represent a significant barrier to entry for potential competitors, as this makes it difficult to remove and replace Qwen with another solution.

Owning the AI Cloud Stack from GPUs to Model Studio

Ultimately, winning the AI race will be determined by serving those workloads at scale. On that dimension, Alibaba Cloud appears well-positioned. Not only does Alibaba Cloud hold a dominant position within China's AI cloud marketplace, but it has also been developing vertically integrated infrastructure for many years, including its Hanguang 800 AI inference chip, which has outperformed all other products on the market (at least, in standard image-recognition testing) since it was first released. The restrictions placed upon Chinese companies purchasing the most advanced Nvidia-based AI chips, as a result of recent U.S.-based export-control regulations, have prompted the Beijing government to encourage domestic alternative hardware development.

Alibaba is also working on creating innovations on the software end to mitigate the negative impacts of a chip shortage. The Aegaeon pooling system from Alibaba uses extremely detailed scheduling so that it can virtualize the use of a GPU for each token. As a result, in internal testing within Model Studio, Alibaba Cloud was able to reduce its usage of Nvidia GPUs by more than 80%, increase its output (useful throughput) by up to nine times, and essentially put the work of almost 1200 GPUs onto approximately 200 GPUs. On top of this layer of infrastructure is Alibaba Cloud Model Studio, a complete development environment. Enterprises can use their own data to finely tune Qwen models using a retrieval-augmented generation, deploy them into a logically segmented, network-based environment, and access a repository of other models such as audio, speech recognition, translation, and text-to-video systems like Qwen TTS, Qwen ASR, and Wan.

Real-World Scale: Taobao, DingTalk, iPhones, and Cars

An AI strategy only truly matters when it reaches end users. Alibaba's unique advantage is a vast portfolio of both consumer and enterprise applications. Its vast ecosystem has the potential to function immediately as a distribution outlet for next-generation AI capabilities. Qwen was first incorporated into DingTalk (the workplace collaboration app) for summarizing meeting discussions and generating automated emails, and subsequently into Tmall Genie smart speakers, and as a shopping assistant on the Taobao e-commerce site.

More recently, Alibaba AI was selected to operate new features for iPhones in China and will be integrated into BMW automobiles; the Qwen2.5 Omni 7B model has also been tuned to run well on mobile phones and laptops. The ability to roll out AI on devices and via remote computing platforms spanning the breadth of messaging, shopping, operating systems, and automobiles provides Alibaba with a uniquely wide range of touchpoints from which it can generate income as AI influences almost every part of daily life.

Alibaba has additionally decided to redesign its flagship Qwen consumer app to be visually and user experience-wise comparable to OpenAI’s flagship chatbot. They have planned to add agentic (autonomous) shopping capabilities to Taobao and other e-commerce platforms. When the new visual design is connected to the large-scale, trillion parameter back-end AI stack together with the "commerce graph" that maps the commercial relationships between all of Alibaba's customers, suppliers, etc., they will build an environment where Alibaba is no longer simply one of many chatbot vendors, but rather the operating system through which hundreds of millions of Chinese consumers find products and complete transactions.

Risks and Challenges: A Competitive, Geopolitical Battlefield

Geopolitics and broader macro cycles also bring additional uncertainty for the company. Export control rules out of the United States have already cut off Alibaba's ability to purchase the top-of-the-line Nvidia chips, and at the same time, regulators in China are taking a much harder line by encouraging the largest players in China to transition away from using Nvidia's specific GPU models for China alone. Meanwhile, Alibaba is warning about a possible bubble developing globally around AI data-center investments, as hyperscalers invest hundreds of billions of dollars in new infrastructure that will likely never reach full utilization. As such, if AI adoption slows either within China or around the world, or if regulations sharply restrict the amount of money that can be earned from this business model, Alibaba may find itself with large amounts of "sunk cost" (i.e., fixed investment), which would result in significantly lower rates of return on invested capital than bulls currently expect.

Another key risk is domestic regulation. Alibaba has firsthand experience with how domestic antitrust actions can interfere with a company’s ability. While Beijing is currently seeking to promote national AI champions, it has approved all of the required public deployments for Qwen; however, as such approvals are subject to change, so too may be the rules governing data usage, type of content allowed, and/or pricing. Lastly, while Qwen has greatly diminished the quality differential between itself and the leaders in the West (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.), those same Western organizations are advancing their models at a rapid pace, and the cross-border competitive arena will impact markets such as global enterprise software, gaming, and scientific computing.

Conclusion

Alibaba’s competitive advantages and the sheer scope of its investments stand out. Most importantly, Qwen is not a solo research effort. Rather, it represents a rapidly evolving product family that is both sufficiently "open" to attract developers from around the world, sufficiently "specialized" to support true enterprise-class workload, and strong enough at the top of the market to compete head-to-head with any other model in the world. Whether through Qwen2.5 Coder, creating future generations of coding assistant tools, or through Qwen3 Omni, immersing users in augmented reality and automotive applications, or through Wan 2.1, producing video at rates that are unmatched for their quality by any other benchmark, Alibaba has developed a comprehensive portfolio of commercialized AI applications that are both wide and deep.

The past ten years were dominated by cloud and mobile. However, the next ten years will be led by AI infrastructure and agents. Based on model quality, infrastructure control, and real-world deployment inside a massive domestic market, Alibaba is one of the handful of companies globally that could credibly argue they have the lead in this space. Regardless of whether you are an investor, developer, or simply an observer of the global tech balance, it is getting increasingly difficult to tell the story of AI in the 2020s without placing Alibaba at or near the center of that story.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chipotle: Huge Turnaround Opportunity Vs. Demise Of A Great Brand
data.description:
The last 16 months weren't great for Chipotle (CMG), to say the least. A PR crisis on meal proportions, the leaving of legendary CEO Brian Niccol, and dropping to the bottom of the leaderboard for industry same-store growth have all combined to make Chipotle one of the worst performers in the S&P 500.

Whatever the cause may be, Chipotle is no longer viewed by investors as a best-in-class business, and it's difficult to argue against it in light of recent results.

The question is whether we're witnessing Chipotle's demise or if it's just a tougher period that will eventually pass. If it's the latter, this might be an extraordinary turnaround investment opportunity.

Let's dive in.

Chipotle's "Year From Hell"

When Bill Simmons says a sports team is having a "year from hell," he typically means that it simply can't catch a break, whether it's injuries, harsh calls, a tough schedule, or bad beats; everything is stacking up against it.

Well, it's difficult to deny that Chipotle has been having a "year from hell," or, more precisely, 16 months from hell.

It started with a PR crisis amid viral social media complaints about meal proportions back in mid-2024. A few months later, legendary CEO Brian Niccol decided to leave for Starbucks (SBUX). Then, it's been mainly a very tough macro environment, persistent quality complaints, and market share losses to price promoters.

All in all, Chipotle could really use a break.

Chipotle's Results Have Been Deteriorating

Over the years, investors grew accustomed to seeing mid-teens revenue growth from Chipotle, alongside margin expansion. The consistency of results is what justified paying a high premium for the stock, as investors and analysts expected to see this trajectory continuing for a very long time.

Well, Q3 was another quarter that put a huge dent in investors' confidence. Revenue increased 7.5% to $3.0 billion, but almost all of it was derived from new store openings, and not all growth is treated equally. Comparable sales of 0.3% turned positive, but it was due to a 1.1% increase in average check, offset by a 0.8% decline in traffic.

With comparable sales growing lower than inflation, and with the company trying to fix its proportion problems, margins had to come down. Restaurant-level margins dropped 100 bps to 24.5%, and operating margins dropped a similar 100 bps to 15.9%.

Looking ahead, Chipotle cut its guidance for comparable sales in 2025, now expecting a decline, and maintained its openings outlook for the year. For 2026, they expect to accelerate store openings, a testament to their confidence in being able to revive same-store growth.

All in all, it's hard to underwrite a mid-teens revenue growth & high-teens EPS growth future when results look like this today. It requires a leap of faith and confidence that investors don't seem to have.

Chipotle Is Losing Ground To Competitors

It seems that a budget-conscious consumer is turning to promotions or home cooking, and a higher-income consumer is turning to DoorDash (DASH) and Uber Eats (UBER) for a more diversified lunch rotation. Either way, Chipotle is ranking quite low in the sector.

I do think it's worth noting that Chipotle is dealing with tougher comps than most of its peers, and it could very well be the reason for its much inferior performance. It will be interesting to see how it ranks in future quarters, especially starting Q1-26, when comps get much easier.

Efforts To Turn Things Around Haven't Moved The Needle So Far

Chipotle hasn't been resting on its laurels in wait for things to turn around. It seems that management is determined to do everything in its power to change Chipotle's fortunes.

They increased marketing activity heavily on TV and other platforms. They are also doing many more campaigns than they used to, with colleges, service members, holidays, and rewards members.

They've also been pushing more menu innovation, planning to increase the cadence of limited-time offerings, and launching more extras, including the recently announced Red Chimichurri dip.

What they haven't been doing, and perhaps they will have to eventually, is offering value meals or discounts. The only thing that came close to it is their new group order option, which was released with a $10 coupon, but that's far from the $7 value meals its competitors are offering.

For now, I think it makes sense for management to try and preserve their brand image as an always-on value proposition. Once you start doing these value campaigns, it's difficult to stop.

However, if they continue to bleed share, they might have to relent on that front.

Valuation & History Are The Main Things To Rely On

I think that the idea of a relatively healthy, fresh, tasty, satisfying, and quick meal for $13 remains quite appealing. I believe that this is a category that has seen increased competition, but it's still gaining share overall, and I expect this will continue.

Aside from this qualitative argument that, at some point, Chipotle should regain popularity, investors are left leaning on valuation.

At $34 a share, Chipotle trades at 29 times forward earnings and 28 times next year's estimates, which are quite underwhelming as they bake in just 5% growth in 2026.

This places Chipotle at a valuation level that's not too far above much more mature peers. If and when comparable sales growth accelerates to 2% or higher, Chipotle is likely to return to 15%+ EPS growth and fetch a much higher multiple.

Until it happens, though, I think we can argue that it reached some type of valuation floor, that is, if things don't deteriorate further.

Conclusion

Chipotle has been having a year from hell, and I don't envy its new CEO entering right when things got very tough.

I think that as long as the macro picture doesn't turn worse, things shouldn't deteriorate further for Chipotle, providing a valuation floor.

Meanwhile, I believe Q1-26 is when things will start to look much better, at which point both consensus estimates and the valuation multiple are likely to expand.

As such, I think the current risk/reward is quite attractive, and reiterate Chipotle at a "Buy."
